raw_response:
## Overall assessment

The HR Security Policy partially supports SOC 2 CC1.4 because it includes pre-employment screening, onboarding security awareness, acceptable use acknowledgment, access review upon transfer, and termination procedures. However, it is primarily a personnel security policy and does not fully demonstrate that Meridian Analytics attracts, develops, evaluates, and retains competent personnel in alignment with business and security objectives.

An auditor would likely flag the following gaps.

| # | Finding title | Related section | Why it is a gap | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | No defined competency or qualification requirements | Sections 1, 2, 3 | The policy does not define required competencies, qualifications, skills, certifications, education, or experience for roles, especially security-sensitive or technical positions. SOC 2 CC1.4 expects the organization to demonstrate that personnel are selected and retained based on competence aligned with objectives. Reference and employment checks alone do not establish competency requirements. | High |
| 2 | Hiring process does not address role-based screening depth | Section 3 — Pre-Employment Screening | The policy requires identity verification, reference checks, and employment history verification, but it does not define enhanced screening for higher-risk roles, such as privileged IT administrators, finance personnel, security staff, or employees with access to sensitive customer data. There is no mention of criminal background checks, education verification, professional license/certification verification, or risk-based screening criteria where legally permitted. | Medium |
| 3 | No documented process for evaluating candidate competence | Section 3 — Pre-Employment Screening | The policy does not describe how hiring managers assess whether candidates are competent for the role. There is no mention of structured interviews, technical assessments, skills validation, documented hiring criteria, or approval of role requirements before hiring. This weakens evidence that the company attracts and selects competent individuals. | High |
| 4 | Security awareness training frequency is not defined | Section 4.2 — Ongoing Training | The policy states that employees must complete security awareness training “on an ongoing basis,” but it does not specify the required cadence, such as annually, quarterly, or upon material policy changes. Without a defined frequency, compliance is difficult to measure and enforce. | Medium |
| 5 | No tracking, enforcement, or escalation process for training completion | Section 4 — Security Awareness Training | The policy requires training but does not state how completion is tracked, who monitors compliance, what reports are reviewed, or what happens when employees fail to complete training on time. Auditors would expect evidence of completion monitoring and escalation for overdue training. | High |
| 6 | No role-based training requirements | Section 4 — Security Awareness Training | The training described is general security awareness. The policy does not require role-specific training for personnel with elevated responsibilities, such as developers, system administrators, customer support, incident responders, HR personnel, or managers. CC1.4 expects individuals to be developed to perform their assigned responsibilities competently. | High |
| 7 | Training content review cadence is vague | Section 4.3 — Training Content | The policy states that training content is reviewed “when updates are deemed necessary,” but it does not define who makes that determination, how often content must be reviewed, or what triggers an update. This may result in stale training content that does not reflect current threats, systems, or policy changes. | Low |
| 8 | No performance management or competency evaluation process | Not addressed | The policy does not describe ongoing performance evaluations, competency assessments, goal setting, manager feedback, remediation of performance issues, or periodic review of whether employees remain qualified for their roles. CC1.4 includes evaluating competence and addressing shortcomings. | High |
| 9 | No professional development or continuing education program | Not addressed | The policy does not address career development, continuing education, certifications, skills development, mentoring, or training plans beyond security awareness. This is a gap against the “develop” component of CC1.4. | Medium |
| 10 | No retention strategy or workforce planning | Not addressed | The policy does not address retention of competent personnel, succession planning, backup coverage for key roles, employee engagement, compensation alignment, or mitigation of key-person dependency. CC1.4 expects the organization to demonstrate commitment to retaining competent individuals, not only screening and terminating them. | Medium |
| 11 | Role changes focus only on access, not competence or training needs | Section 6 — Role Changes and Transfers | The policy requires an access review when an employee transfers roles, but it does not require review of new competency requirements, completion of role-specific training, manager approval of qualifications, or update of job responsibilities. Employees could move into sensitive roles without documented validation of competence. | Medium |
| 12 | Contractor competence and onboarding requirements are not sufficiently detailed | Sections 2, 3, 4, 5 | The policy states that contractors are in scope, but it does not clearly define whether contractors are subject to the same screening, training, acceptable use acknowledgment, role-based training, or competency validation as employees. Third parties with system access may present similar or greater risk. | Medium |
| 13 | No evidence retention requirements for CC1.4 activities | Sections 3, 4, 5 | The policy mentions retaining identity documents and AUP acknowledgments, but it does not define retention requirements for reference check documentation, employment verification results, training records, performance reviews, role-specific training, or competency assessments. Auditors need evidence that the controls operated. | Medium |
| 14 | Acceptable Use Policy acknowledgment is only required before initial access | Section 5.1 — Acceptable Use Policy Acknowledgment | The policy requires employees and contractors to sign the AUP before system access, but it does not require periodic re-acknowledgment after policy updates or at least annually. This is more directly related to security awareness and control environment expectations, but it also supports employee accountability and competence. | Low |
| 15 | Termination access revocation window may be insufficient for involuntary terminations | Section 7.1 — Access Revocation | The policy allows up to 24 hours to revoke all logical and physical access. For involuntary or high-risk terminations, auditors may expect immediate revocation at or before notification. While this is more directly relevant to logical access controls, it may be flagged as a personnel security weakness. | Medium |

## Summary conclusion

The policy provides a useful baseline for personnel security, but it does not fully satisfy the broader intent of SOC 2 CC1.4. The main gaps are the absence of documented competency requirements, role-based hiring and training practices, performance evaluation, professional development, retention planning, and evidence retention. To strengthen readiness, Meridian Analytics should supplement this policy with formal hiring, performance management, training, and workforce development procedures that demonstrate personnel are competent and remain competent for their assigned responsibilities.
